speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 6. Survivor and war-crimes trials participant Lawrence Rhee. The date is April 9, 1996. Would you kindly introduce who's sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] This is my wife, Alicia. [LAUGH] And-- [CLEARING THROAT] excuse me. And we are the happiest couple in the world. We have been married for 45 years. We have four wonderful children, seven grandchildren. And it has just been one great life with her and the family. Nobody could ask for more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself in Munich, sitting on the ruins of Hitler's headquarters in ruin, the Braunes Haus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945, I think, early '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- friend. [LAUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my master sergeant Herman Mendels and myself, in a dugout, a few hours after our captain got hit right in this dugout.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the dugout, and what--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was right on the Siegfried Line-- within a few hundred yards, actually, of the Siegfried Line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This again, during the campaign-- '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you, in this picture-- on the top, or the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the bottom. That's me, and that's Herman. Herman is the one that found his wife in the Netherlands. And she had been a passenger on the infamous the boat that was refused entrance and unloading the passengers everywhere, except the Netherlands took it over at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of Herman Mendels and myself, showing in the background the destroyed Siegfried Line-- or parts of the destroyed Siegfried Line.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same time, during the campaign in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of myself in the little Alsatian village of Zutzendorf, standing by my jeep, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same time-- in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my wife and our four children. It was a copy of the passport picture when we came back to the United States, in 1959. And I've carried in my pocketbook ever since. I think the state of the picture speaks for itself. [LAUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say your childrens' names? Like, who is in the back, in the very far back?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the very far back, it's Norman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the next row, on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The left, it's Robert and Else Arlene, and Lillian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Alicia in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a the picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my youngest daughter, Else Arlene, with her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Easpario. Mike Easpario.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 Years ago, on their wedding day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right here in Albuquerque.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my son, then-Captain Robert, with his wife, Nancy, in Kokomo, Indiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture or?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of our oldest son, Norman, with his wife, Vera, in Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter Lillian with her husband, Larry Mowery. And this picture was taken in Albuquerque in 1989.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture our oldest granddaughter. And her name is Christina. She lives here in Albuquerque.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of Gregory. And he lives in Kansas City.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson. Son of our youngest daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of our granddaughter Elizabeth. And she lives in Kokomo, Indiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Dave. We call him David. And he lives right here in Albuquerque.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Becky. This granddaughter lives in Kansas City.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our granddaughter Suzanne, and she lives in Kokomo, Indiana.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our grandson David. We have two Davids in the family. And he lives in Kansas City.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just the envelope. A document was in there, but is-- Lord Justice Lawrence sent me some papers. And you can see my name on there. And the top, it says 'In His Majesty's service.' It's just something I wanted to keep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the very beginning of the trials. I don't know exactly what date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the American chief prosecutor, the former Supreme Court Justice Robert Jackson. He was the chief prosecutor for the American prosecution. He gave me this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that his autograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's his autograph, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does the autograph say? Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: 'To Lawrence L. Rhee, with best wishes, Robert Jackson.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the 20 defendants. Unfortunately, you can only see Goering's belly, right behind the first guard on the left. I took this picture clandestinely out of the projection booth, and I could not move around anymore. But there you have the 20 defendants-- and their guards, with the white helmets, surrounding them. The picture is of the 20 defendants seated in two rows in the box, there, in front of the guards-- the fellow with the white helmets on. They were always under guard and well watched.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are these signatures?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the back of the same picture. And the defendants signed it, except three of them. Hjalmar Schacht, von Papen, and Goering didn't want to sign, as they said, with the rest of the hoodlums, on the same paper. Unquote.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get these signatures?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no personal contacts with the prisoners, but, through the guard that was Heinrich Hoffmann's guard-- and he had his buddies watching the prisoners-- they managed to get these signatures for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of the eight judges. They signed it for me, through contacts. Again, I didn't have connections with them. But I got it through friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was at Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is at Nuremberg, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: First trial.

speaker: interviewee
text: First trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Hitler's personal photographer, Heinrich Hoffmann. When he signed it, he only signed it, first, Heinrich Hoffmann. Then I asked him, well, how about me? My name is on there. And, hesitantly, he wrote 'To Mr. Rhee.' And by the way, Heinrich Hoffmann was the person who taught me some about photography. I learned photography from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Hitler, Frau Goering-- or Mrs. Goering-- and their daughter Edda. Heinrich Hoffmann gave me these pictures. And, as far as I know, these are the only ones in the world. There are several of them you will see later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the same group, another setting-- of the same series This is the back of the last picture you saw. And you see it's copyright from Heinrich Hoffmann's photo collection. All these pictures have Heinrich Hoffmann's stamp on the back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the same group again, just a different pose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's Hitler, Frau Goering, little Edda Goering, and she's being held by the nanny, who holds her in her arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, there is Hitler, Frau Goering, and Edda Goering.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is in Yugoslavia. All the next pictures you'll see were taken by the Germans and freely distributed in Yugoslavia, to keep the population scared. You see here two German soldiers having a great time, looking at the corpse of the guy that had been hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were given to me by the Yugoslav prosecution staff. They needed a certain amount of copies for all the judges, for all the defense lawyers and prosecution and so forth and so on. And when they handed them to have the copies made, I asked them, how about making a copy for myself? And this fellow said, go right ahead. Help yourself to all you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a mass hanging. And, as you can see the people, in the background had to attend. Everybody in any village where mass hangings took place always had to attend.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another mass hanging. And again, the population was made to watch it. And I want to call your attention of the little children in the background, there, who also had to watch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans took it, starting in 1941 until the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then this was handed over to the Yugoslavians by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Yugoslav prosecution was able to pick it up from the population and present it as evidence. Because German photographers took these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of a corpse being used as a tetherball by some guys who didn't have anything better to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another mass hanging. What I would like to call your attention to is the Hitler or German guy in the background, giving his Heil Hitler salute, in the back, there-- behind the tree. With a big smile on his face.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of the warfare between the two political parties in Yugoslavia during the time of the German occupation. They had the Chetniks, and they had the Partisans. And one of them had been caught-- in this picture, I don't know who is who, but one of them had been caught by the other party. And there is an execution with a knife in his hand, at the throat of the victim. And the other one is the one who reads off the accusations. And the third one is the judge who's condemning him to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is another mass hanging. But I want to call your attention to the German army girl that's in the back, there, and having just one awful good time. Look at the smile on her face.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a woman who had been literally beaten to a pulp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yugoslavia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her whole body was beaten--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was just beaten to pulp. Look at her. And shot in the head, too, as you can see the bullet hole in the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: A person literally beaten to a pulp. They're practically unrecognizable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a beheading machine. The Germans didn't want to bury all the corpses, so a fellow with a lot of ingenuity invented this guillotine. The body was strapped to this, and the knife or sword was lowered. The head would fall onto one side, into the river, and the body down the other side, and that way they didn't have to bury them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture or?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of an underground who was captured. They caught him-- cut the Star of David into his cheek.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a woman was first beaten. Her breast was cut off. And finally she was shot to death. Again, in Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is that of a young underground warrior who was captured, was condemned to death, to die, but defying to the last. See how he raises his hands in defiance. He's standing on a stool that will be shortly kicked out from under him. Also, watch all the German army uniforms in the background, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yugoslavia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yugoslavia

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just another picture of a person being hanged. See the two German soldiers, doing the last touch-up before kicking out the stool or ladder.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Yugoslavia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of an announcement, both in German and in Yugoslav or whatever you call the particular local language there. It said that a German army truck with one officer, two noncoms, and one enlisted man was attacked. The four passengers were killed. In retaliation, 400 Yugoslav people were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a beheading of a prisoner or whatever they have you want to call it. You see how two guys are holding his arm, and the third one is delivering the blow to the neck, with an axe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the people in the background?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is of the same beheading. You can see the axe is down on the neck. And I want especially call to your attention the SS insignia on the guy that's holding one of the arms of the victim. The lapel shows it quite clearly. Or lapel, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the same beheading, it seems. Again, you see the guy just lowering his axe. He has it raised, but it's ready to be lowered on the neck of the victim, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the same victim, or victims. There are two heads. On the-- heads in the grass. Somebody put a cigarette into the mouth of one of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the same heads of the previous picture. They were displayed publicly. They were picked up from the grass and displayed publicly. And everybody, of course, had to march by and look at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was, again, in Yugoslavia?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all Yugoslavia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a victim that was literally beaten to pulp. Just look at the shoulder or whatever used to be. There's absolutely nothing left of the victim except a soft mass.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, again, is Yugoslavia. An underground fighter was caught. Somehow he got away and, after a short while, was recaptured. No, just a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get these group of pictures that you show next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, these were given to me by the Yugoslav prosecution staff at the Nuremberg trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: After a short while, this person was recaptured and led away. You can see the German soldiers all around him, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows that the population of this particular village was forced go to this big, wide field. And you see some of the people of this village or whatever it was, standing there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next on the scene were two German soldiers with their German shepherd dogs. On this picture, you see that the dogs had been sicced on the victim, and they're physically attacking the victim. After the dogs had killed the victim, they threw his corpse to the pigs. The same thing. The pigs are eating the corpse. This is the gentle way of executing a guy-- simply tie him to a pole and shoot him in the head or in the neck-- whatever it was. And this is the same execution. You can see the two Germans standing there and looking at the victim. And, still in Yugoslavia, the same execution. And looks like they give him the coup de grace. All in Yugoslavia, of course. Again, Yugoslavia. These two victims, there, were first burned and then hanged. Again, Yugoslavia. This guy, as far as I can tell, was hanged. You can see the hands tied to his back. Again Yugoslavia. The soldiers of occupation didn't have much of a chance for target practice. So what they would do is round up a bunch of people, ran them through the field, and shot at them on the run. This was called 'the rabbit run.' This is the result. You can see the bodies strewn all over the field. And, as I said already, they called this 'the rabbit run'-- target practice.
